explanation: Use 'an' before words that start with a vowel sound.
Use 'a' before words that start with a consonant sound.
The correct collocation is 'depend on', not 'depend of'.
The correct collocation is 'consist of', not 'consist in'.
The correct collocation is 'interested in', not 'interested for'.
The correct collocation is 'married to', not 'married with'.
The verb 'listen' requires the preposition 'to'.
Use 'wait for' when referring to waiting for someone or something.
Use 'arrive at' (specific place) or 'arrive in' (city/country), not 'arrive to'.
Use 'explain to me', not 'explain me'.
'Discuss' is a transitive verb — no 'about' needed.
'Enter' is transitive — no 'into' needed (unless figurative).
In conditional sentences, use past simple after 'if', not 'would'.
exampleWrong: If I would know, I would tell you.
exampleCorrect: If I knew, I would tell you.

explanation: Use past tense with 'yesterday', not future tense.
exampleWrong: Yesterday I will go to school.

explanation: Use future tense or 'going to' with 'tomorrow'.
exampleWrong: Tomorrow I went to the store.
exampleCorrect: Tomorrow I will go to the store.

explanation: Use present perfect tense with 'since'.
exampleCorrect: I have lived here for 5 years.

explanation: Use present perfect tense with 'for' + duration.
exampleCorrect: I have studied English for 3 years.

explanation: Use 'do homework', not 'make homework'. 'Make' is for creating/producing.
exampleWrong: I need to make my homework.
exampleCorrect: I need to do my homework.

explanation: 'Make a mistake' is correct! (This is a common collocation.)
Use 'tell me' (tell + person), not 'say me'. 'Say' is followed by words/sentences.
'Tell me' is more natural than 'say to me' in most contexts.
Use 'take a photo/picture', not 'do a photo'.
Use 'do sport' or 'play sports', not 'make sport'.
You 'beat' an opponent, but 'win' a game/competition.
'Teach' means to instruct others. 'Learn' is what students do.
'Actually' means 'in fact', not 'now'. Russian 'актуально' ≠ English 'actually'.
exampleWrong: Actually I work on a project.
exampleCorrect: Currently I am working on a project.

explanation: English 'sympathetic' means showing sympathy, not 'likeable' (Russian 'симпатичный').
'Accurate' means precise/exact, not 'neat' (Russian 'аккуратный').
exampleWrong: He is a very accurate person.
exampleCorrect: He is a very neat person.

explanation: 'Better' is already comparative. Don't add 'more'.
'Worse' is already comparative. Don't add 'more'.
'Best' is already superlative. Don't add 'most'.
exampleWrong: This is the most best option.

explanation: Short adjectives form comparatives with -er, not with 'more'.
exampleWrong: He is more taller than me.

explanation: 'Every' is always followed by a singular noun.
Use 'many' with countable plurals, 'much' with uncountable nouns.
Use 'much' with uncountable nouns, not 'many'.
'Information' is uncountable — no plural form and no article 'a/an'.
'Advice' is uncountable — use 'a piece of advice' for one item.
exampleCorrect: He gave me a piece of advice.

explanation: 'Furniture' is uncountable — no plural form.
'Luggage' is uncountable — no plural form.
exampleCorrect: I have two pieces of luggage.

explanation: Third person singular requires 'doesn't', not 'don't'.
Third person singular requires 'doesn't', not 'don't'.
Third person singular requires 'doesn't', not 'don't'.
First person 'I' requires 'don't', not 'doesn't'.
Plural subjects require 'don't', not 'doesn't'.
'We' requires 'don't', not 'doesn't'.
After 'does', use the base form of the verb (no -s/-es).
After 'did', use the base form of the verb.
Abbreviations like Mr., Mrs., Dr. typically use a period in American English.
Don't use 'the' before possessive determiners (my, your, his, etc.).
